message:
fix(snapshot): integrity check + docs alignment (ft-ybtyg)

Closes ft-ybtyg. Two related gaps in the session-persistence trust
story:

1. Four restore-path INSERTs at session_restore.rs wrote the literal
   string `'restore'` into session_checkpoints.state_hash. The column
   was never a real integrity witness — the hash was a placeholder
   that no later load path could verify against.

2. Docstring drift: snapshot_engine.rs:14 and storage.rs:609 both
   advertised "BLAKE3 hash" while the actual `compute_state_hash`
   helper at snapshot_engine.rs uses
   `std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher` — SipHash-24 formatted
   as 16 hex chars of u64. Misleading to auditors.

## Fix

Conservative: keep SipHash-24 on disk (no schema migration, no
dependency shift), swap the hardcoded placeholder for a real
computed hash, and align the docs to reality.

Added `compute_restore_state_hash(session_id, pane_id_map, now_ms)`
in `session_restore.rs`. Deterministic SipHash-24 over a canonical
serialization of the exact inputs the restore INSERT consumes:
- domain separator `b"ft-ybtyg:restore-checkpoint-v1"` so the
  hash cannot collide with `snapshot_engine::compute_state_hash`
  even for identical u64 outputs,
- session_id,
- now_ms,
- pane_id_map sorted by old_id (HashMap iteration order is
  non-deterministic; sort before hashing).

Wired into both `save_restore_checkpoint` (test-gated helper) and
`finalize_restore` (production path). The two other `'restore'`
literal occurrences at lines 2320 + 2641 are in test fixtures that
intentionally construct legacy-format rows; those stay unchanged
as a back-compat regression corpus.

Docs realigned in lockstep:
- snapshot_engine.rs:14 now says "SipHash-24 (16-hex-char u64)"
  with an ft-ybtyg pointer to `compute_restore_state_hash`.
- storage.rs:609 schema comment now says "SipHash-24 (16-hex-char
  u64) ... Not a cryptographic integrity hash" so future readers
  don't assume BLAKE3-grade properties.

## Tests (8 new)

Inline in `session_restore::tests`:
- `compute_restore_state_hash_is_deterministic` — same inputs →
  same output across three repeated calls.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_is_16_hex_chars` — output shape pin:
  exactly 16 lowercase hex digits.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_not_the_literal_restore` — guards
  against accidental regression to the pre-fix sentinel.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_differs_by_session_id` — hash
  discriminates on session_id.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_differs_by_pane_map` — hash
  discriminates on the new pane id mapping.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_differs_by_timestamp` — hash
  discriminates on checkpoint_at.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_insertion_order_independent` — the
  HashMap-sort step must neutralize iteration-order differences.
- `compute_restore_state_hash_domain_separated_from_dedup_hash` —
  the `ft-ybtyg:` domain prefix must make the restore hash
  distinct from a naive session-id-only SipHash.

## Non-changes

- On-disk format preserved (column is still TEXT, still 16 hex
  chars). No schema migration, no existing-DB breakage.
- No new dependency (SipHash comes from std; BLAKE3 would require
  a new crate pull).
- Public API unchanged (`compute_restore_state_hash` is
  `pub(super)`).

## Out of scope

- Full load-time integrity verification (recompute-on-read and
  reject-on-mismatch). The helper is in place; wiring the
  verifier into `load_latest_checkpoint` is a follow-up because
  it requires coordinating with the existing dedup-skip semantics
  that also read state_hash.
- BLAKE3 migration. If/when a future bead decides cryptographic
  integrity is required, that migration can be a separate lane;
  the docstring alignment landed here makes the current state
  honest in the meantime.

## Related

- ft-kegvt (969b9e02) — sanitizer at the exit boundary; closed
  the CR/LF injection vector that could ride a tampered `command`.
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — same trust-boundary
  theme (MCP arbitrary-file R/W).
- ft-xv561 (070117a0) — pattern-engine ReDoS.
